original_taxonomist_notes: PreFlexion; 41/7 prepost myomeres, long preanal length, damaged. Late yolksac/early postyolksac. Small size at development excludes other Clupeids and unlikely to be an Engraulid at bypass facility.  Historically a common taxon in Yolo Bypass samples.
qc_notes: Long preanal length and distinctly clupeid. Dorosoma petenense and Alosa sapidissima are the only clupeids recorded from the Delta, and this is much too small to be A. sapidissima.

original_taxonomist_notes: PreFlexion; Distinct taxon to family (very short preanal length, yolk and pigment characters). 1 damaged specimen not measured. Lack of dorsal pigment posterior to occiput is diagnostic to species among Atherinids of system.
qc_notes: Distinct taxon. The Sacramento River Delta has Menidia beryllina and M. audens. Unless there is a good description for M. audens that allows this species to be eliminated (I can't currently find one), this ID should be left at genus.

original_taxonomist_notes: PreFlexion; 9/19 prepost myomeres and short preanal length. Distinctly a topminnow and too short at development for Gambusia. Either Fundulus or Lucania. F. parvipinnis distribution further south. Lucania parva instroduced to San Francisco Bay. Size at development, myomeres, and pigmentaton consistent.

original_taxonomist_notes: Meristics consistent with M. berylina, which is a very common non-native silverside in Sacramento-San Joquin system. Prominent silver/dark stripe and scale cross-hatching is prominent, which generally distinguishes from other native more estuarine/marine topsmelt/jacksmelts.
qc_notes: Anal fin ray count: I;16 1st Dorsal: IV. 2nd Dorsal: I;8. All features consistent with M. beryllina, and exclude native smelt species.

Only 2 liverbearers in Sacramento/San Joaquin system, G. affinis  and Lucania parva. Dorsally flattened head and anal fin insertion anterior of dorsal fin are diagnostic of G. affinis…a distinct taxon.

O. mykiss (steelhead/rainbow) and O. tshawytscha (Chinook) most common salmonids in Sacramento system.  Parr marks approx equal to space btn parr marks, 13-14 dorsal fin rays, and 19 anal fin rays fits chinook better than steelhead. Also, the small size of these specimens is not consisistent with steelhead that would be expected to be larger outmigrating smolts if collected at Yolo Bypass. More likely outmigrating Chinook fry.  Note: both specimens have what appear to be clean straight clips of caudal fin, which would be odd for marking fish of this size. Maybe facility damage or clipped for genetic testing when collected at facility?

qc_notes: Vial label says Cyprinidae, but spreadsheet indicates Clupeidae and I agree with clupeid. Specimen is just under 5 mm with no yolk, and preanal myomere counts are only approximate, but appear to be ~36, which confirms to D. petenense.
D2:19. A: 17. Confirms to C. asper.

original_taxonomist_notes: 63% PAL, 9 dors rays, 10 anal rays, apparent symmetrical caudal fin, small (terminial?) mouth, heavy pigment on occiput. Myomere counts appear to be approx  45 but somewhat obscured by pigment and development. Final ID based on total myomere count of 45 and pigmentation.  No caudal spot at length excludes blackfish and hitch and roach (as do other characters).
qc_notes: Origin of dorsal fin is anterior to origin of pelvic fin, which excludes M. conocephalus. Dorsal counts appear to be 10-11; anal counts 10. M. conocephalus have fin ray counts of D: 8 & A: 8-9. Counts confirm to O. microlepidotus much more consistently.
